text: The surgeon performs immediate lateral dissection following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) close to their origin.
The surgeon continues to develop the medial lateral plane, carefully dissecting in front of the uterine fascia, with plans to fully mobilize the splenic flexure.
The surgeon focuses on pelvic dissection following chronic mobilization, aiming to achieve total mesorectal excision (TME).
The surgeon performs posterior dissection, carefully navigating between the hypogastric nerves and staying close to the specimen to facilitate the procedure.
The robotic instrument is angulated deep in the pelvis, allowing precise dissection at this deeper level, enhancing visibility and control within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon completes posterior mobilization of the rectum, then shifts focus to the pelvic sidewalls, carefully avoiding damage to the clearly visible hypogastric nerves.
The robotic second hand applies gentle traction to the tissue, demonstrating the necessary force for effective manipulation during pelvic dissection.
The surgeon makes a decision at the level of the Crouch of Douglas, positioned a centimeter or two above the posterior wall of the vagina, to advance the dissection anteriorly.
The second robotic left hand provides counter-attraction, enhancing the visibility of the surgical field during the dissection.
The surgeon uses gentle counter-traction to perform precise resection behind the posterior wall of the vagina, progressing the dissection further on each side of the rectum.
The surgeon makes a conscious decision to proceed with an extra-fascial dissection to achieve an R-zero resection, as observed in the surgical field.
A piece of precycled fascia is left intact with the specimen, ensuring complete R-zero resection for subsequent histological examination.
The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the endopelvic fascia surrounding the rectal tube. The rectum is fully mobilized before being divided using a 45mm endo-GIA stapler with two firings.
The empty pelvis is visible with intact pre-sacral nerves and fascia. The surgical specimen is extracted through the left liposa.
